evidence: Top-level packages map cleanly to domain capabilities: api/ (HTTP), config/ (grammar), exploration/ (algorithms), model/ (DTOs), query/ (Lucene execution), indexer/ (corpus ingestion), utils/ (shared math/CQL), viz/ (rendering)
Package-private access control used effectively to hide internal collaborators: CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer, all *Handlers classes are package-private

ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, Edge all reside in model/ — but ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions reside in exploration/ despite being pure data records with no algorithm logic
model/package-info.java explicitly documents the record-vs-class split and package boundary, showing deliberate design ownership
GrammarConfigSerializer moved from config/ to api/ because it holds fastjson2 dependency — correct placement, documented context provided
high_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: Exploration option records (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) sit in exploration/ while their peer result types (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate) sit in model/. A new developer following model/ as the home for domain value objects would not know to look in exploration/ for the option types.

evidence: All HTTP handler classes use plural -Handlers suffix uniformly: SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers
Utility classes use -Utils suffix consistently: CqlUtils, MathUtils, RelationUtils, HttpApiUtils — except LogDiceCalculator which uses -Calculator
Java naming conventions (camelCase methods, PascalCase types, SCREAMING_SNAKE constants) applied uniformly throughout all packages
Record types used consistently for pure value objects; non-record classes documented in model/package-info.java with explicit rationale
Package-private visibility applied consistently to implementation classes across api/, exploration/ packages
Enum label() + toString() override pattern applied consistently across RelationEdgeType, SharingCategory, PosGroup
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: LogDiceCalculator uses a -Calculator suffix while CqlUtils, MathUtils, RelationUtils, HttpApiUtils all use -Utils, creating a single naming style island in the utils package.

evidence: HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling consistently maps IAE→400, RequestEntityTooLargeException→413, IOException→500 for all API endpoints — uniform top-level error boundary

config/ layer: GrammarConfigLoader throws IAE for validation failures, IOException for I/O failures, IllegalStateException in createDefaultEnglish() — three distinct exception types for three distinct failure modes, documented in Javadoc
query/ layer: QueryExecutor interface declares throws IOException for all methods; CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency wraps RuntimeException in IOException to normalise unexpected failures
Nullable returns in utils/ and config/ are annotated with @Nullable (CqlUtils.extractConstraintAttribute, RelationConfig.computeDeprel) — contract communicated at type level
ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples degrades silently to proximity fallback when relation not found, logging a warning and setting response field 'fallback: true'
GrammarConfig.getRelation() returns Optional<RelationConfig>; RelationConfig.computeDeprel() returns @Nullable String — two patterns for 'not found' in the same config layer

QueryExecutor method names (executeCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeBcqlQuery, executeDependencyPatternWithPos) are precise and disambiguate four distinct query modes
Exploration API (exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed, compareCollocateProfiles, fetchExamples) communicates operation and mode clearly
buildBaseExploreResponse in ExplorationHandlers — 'base' is vague; the method builds a response map template that callers then augment with seed-specific fields
executeAndFormatCollocations in SketchHandlers — compound name signals two responsibilities; returns Optional<ExecutedSketch> which is not suggested by 'execute'
computeCollocateIntersection in MultiSeedExplorer — the method also builds DiscoveredNoun/CoreCollocate structures and returns a full ExplorationResult; the name understates its scope
handleVisualRadial in VisualizationHandlers — 'Visual' is redundant given the class name VisualizationHandlers

QueryExecutor interface has one implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) — Javadoc explicitly acknowledges this as a testability seam, not an abstraction boundary: 'The single production implementation means the interface acts primarily as a testability seam'
SemanticFieldExplorer documented as coordination facade delegating multi-seed to MultiSeedExplorer and comparison to CollocateProfileComparator — delegation pays for itself in focused ownership

ExploreResponseAssembler separates model-to-JSON translation from both HTTP handling and model objects — clear single responsibility, multiple callers (populateExploreResponse, populateComparisonResponse, buildEdges)
ExplorationHandlers.buildBaseExploreResponse takes Map<String,Object> extraParams as a catch-all for caller-specific fields — bypasses type safety for extended response parameters

SemanticFieldExplorer package-private overload exploreByPattern(String, String, String, String, SingleSeedExplorationOptions) has 5 parameters — documented as testing seam to avoid full grammar config in unit tests
abstraction_fitness sub-axes from mechanical scan: abstraction_leverage=100, indirection_cost=0, interface_honesty=92, delegation_density=100, definition_directness=100, type_discipline=100
pom.xml declares exactly six runtime dependency groups: blacklab (2 artifacts), fastjson2 (1), slf4j-api (1), logback-classic (1, runtime-scoped), jspecify (1) — no overlap or redundancy
Both blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser use property ${blacklab.version}=4.0.0 — version consistency enforced at the POM level
Single JSON library fastjson2 2.0.57 throughout: all JSON serialization and deserialization uses com.alibaba.fastjson2 — no competing JSON libraries detected
slf4j-api + logback-classic is the canonical logging pair; logback correctly scoped as runtime-only dependency
jspecify 1.0.0 for @NonNull/@Nullable annotations — lightweight purpose-built library (no Guava or Lombok just for annotations)
BlackLab raw GitHub repository URL noted in pom.xml comment with documented mitigation options (local install, private mirror) — pragmatic constraint documented in place
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: BlackLab resolved from a raw GitHub URL (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/.../mvn-repo/) rather than Maven Central or a stable private registry. The pom.xml comment acknowledges this with mitigation options, but the dependency remains fragile for CI environments and collaborative development. (Permanent wontfix per review scope.)

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern phases extracted into focused helpers: fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates — each does one thing
CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList uses a documented single-pass accumulation with explicit comment explaining why variance requires a second pass

CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens: outer while loop with inner while loop for bracket tracking; quoted strings inside brackets handled by a third nested level; multiple i-increment paths across branches including explicit i++ inside the ch=='"' branch followed by the implicit inner i++ at the bottom of the inner loop
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() calls Pattern.compile('deprel=["\']([^"\']+)["\']') on every invocation — no static final field

CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest: top-parsing uses double null check: 'Integer top = obj.get("top") != null ? obj.getIntValue("top") : null; int resolvedTop = (top != null && top > 0) ? top : 10;'
SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String,Object>> callback for the response-building step — functional but non-idiomatic Java orchestration
MultiSeedExplorer.buildCoreCollocates contains a stream-in-loop: per collocate, streams all per-seed collocate lists to compute average logDice — O(seeds × collocates) but acceptable at domain scale
ExplorationHandlers → SemanticFieldExplorer handoff: handlers parse and validate params, construct typed option records, call explorer — clean explicit boundary with no implicit state
SemanticFieldExplorer → MultiSeedExplorer/CollocateProfileComparator: thin documented delegation; facade Javadoc explains why each collaborator exists

ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse(Map<String,Object>, ExplorationResult) mutates the passed map rather than returning a new map — the partial map is built by the caller, passed in, and side-effect-completed

ExploreResponseAssembler.populateComparisonResponse takes Map<String,Object> response, ComparisonResult result, Set<String> seeds, int topCollocates, double minLogDice — seeds, topCollocates, minLogDice are HTTP-layer parameters passed into a presentation-assembly method that should only depend on ComparisonResult

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String,Object>> builder callback — makes the data flow through the seam non-linear and harder to follow than a direct extraction loop
WordSketchApiServer constructor injection of handlers is clean — all handler dependencies resolved at construction time with no post-construction mutation

GrammarConfigLoader private helper Javadocs mostly restate method names: '/** Extracts and validates the 'version' field from the root JSON object. */' for parseAndValidateVersion; '/** Parses all relation entries from the JSON root... */' for parseRelations — these add no insight beyond the signature

LogDiceCalculator.relativeFrequency Javadoc: '/** Compute relative frequency (collocate frequency / headword frequency). */' — the formula in parentheses slightly expands on the method name but is formulaic
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() recompiles Pattern.compile on every call — local variable instead of static final field is a code-generation hallmark

CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList: guard 'if (scores.length == 0) { maxScore = 0.0; minScore = 0.0; }' after loop that handles empty arrays through NEGATIVE_INFINITY/POSITIVE_INFINITY sentinels — defensive cleanup that partly duplicates the sentinel initialisation
HttpApiUtils class Javadoc explains at length why two concerns coexist in one class — this is genuine design documentation, not restating
SemanticFieldExplorer class Javadoc with example algorithm trace, h2 sections, design rationale — purposeful and adds real developer value for a complex algorithm class
No pass-through wrappers that add no logic beyond forwarding; SemanticFieldExplorer facade delegates are explicitly documented and add a stable API boundary
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: GrammarConfigLoader private helper Javadocs and the Pattern.compile local variable in RelationConfig.computeDeprel() show minor AI-generation hallmarks: ceremonial documentation and missed static field extraction.

evidence: WordSketchApiServer line 95: '// @deprecated - legacy alias for /api/semantic-field/compare; kept for backward compatibility.' — line comment deprecation on the route registration, not a @Deprecated annotation on the handler method reference
ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc: 'Also reachable at the legacy path /api/semantic-field' — correctly documents the dual-path existence
WordSketchApiServer class Javadoc lists '- GET  /api/semantic-field  - Semantic field comparison (legacy alias)' in the endpoint table — legacy status communicated in docs
GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys rejects 'copulas' key with IAE — active migration guard preventing old-format configs, not residue

QueryResults confirmed in model/ only, GrammarConfigSerializer in api/ only, MathUtils.round2dp() replacing roundTo2DecimalPlaces — all migrations from recent context are complete with no old class stubs remaining
No stale TODO/FIXME comments referencing 'migrate', 'legacy', 'old api', or 'remove after' found in src/main/java/**/*.java
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: The legacy /api/semantic-field route is marked only with a // line comment, not with a @Deprecated annotation on the handler method. IDE tooling and static analysis will not surface this deprecation signal to developers who extend or test the handler.

evidence: model/ holds 11 domain-model types as pure DTOs or immutable objects with no HTTP/IO deps — cohesive and navigable
api/ holds 11 production classes all scoped to HTTP handling (handlers + utilities + server) — at the threshold but cohesive
config/ cleanly separates data (GrammarConfig, RelationConfig) from loading machinery (GrammarConfigLoader) and helpers (RelationPatternBuilder, RelationUtils)
exploration/ holds exactly the 6 types that together own the semantic-field algorithms; dependencies flow cleanly to query/ and model/
ExploreResponseAssembler and GrammarConfigSerializer are both public in api/ but deal only with model→map/JSON serialization, not with HttpExchange — they sit one concern above pure HTTP handling
RelationType enum in model/ holds both routing strategies (SURFACE, DEP) and semantic types (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) — acknowledged wontfix per T4 notes

GrammarConfigLoader.load Javadoc says '@throws IOException if the file cannot be read or the config is invalid', but validation failures (missing version, duplicate ID) now throw IllegalArgumentException — the @throws clause is stale

QueryExecutor.executeCollocations declares @Nullable String lemma; BlackLabQueryExecutor silently returns empty list when null/empty, matching the Javadoc 'returns an empty list silently if null or empty'
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() is annotated @Nullable and returns null for non-DEP relations — all callers (SketchHandlers) guard against null
ExploreResponseAssembler.serializeEdge uses inline Math.round(edge.weight() * 100.0) / 100.0 while all sibling methods use MathUtils.round2dp() — numerically equivalent but inconsistent
ExplorationHandlers.parseRelationConfig throws IllegalArgumentException for unknown relation OR missing relationType — documented and caught by wrapWithErrorHandling
ConcordanceHandlers.parseConcordanceExamplesRequest renames seed→noun and collocate→adjective — documented with explicit Javadoc on the ConcordanceExamplesRequest record
SketchHandlers.handleRelationQueryForPattern throws IAE with message 'Unknown relation: X' when the relation exists but is the wrong type — error message is misleading

CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList: scores array is built from nounScores.values() which is only populated when at least one noun matched, so scores.length == 0 is unreachable; the 'if (scores.length == 0) { maxScore = 0.0; minScore = 0.0; }' guard is dead code

SketchHandlers.handleRelationQueryForPattern: 'String specificDeprel = parts.length > 2 ? parts[2] : null; if (specificDeprel != null && !specificDeprel.isEmpty())' — !specificDeprel.isEmpty() is redundant since parts[2] is always a non-empty String when present

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: parseSeedSet is called on req.seedsRaw() to get the Set, then seeds.size() < 2 is checked — control flow is clear and properly ordered (nouns_per guard → buildExploreRequest → seed count check)

BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeCqlQuery: 'if (tp == null) return Collections.emptyList()' — TextPattern from a successful CQL parse should not be null; this is a defensive guard against a BlackLab implementation detail
MultiSeedExplorer.identifyCommonCollocates: 'int threshold = Math.min(minShared, seedCount)' caps threshold to seedCount — prevents requesting more shared seeds than exist; clean defensive logic
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens depth-counting logic correctly handles quoted strings inside and outside brackets in a single pass — straightforward to follow
jspecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations are applied throughout api/ and query/ public surfaces (HttpApiUtils, QueryExecutor, BlackLabQueryExecutor, ExplorationHandlers)
Optional<RelationType> on RelationConfig.relationType() rather than @Nullable RelationType — forces callers to handle absence explicitly
sealed interface ConcordanceResult with permits clause (SnippetResult, CollocateResult) gives exhaustive switch coverage

CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex 'to support test subclasses that override methods' — valid test seam, but the annotation suggests null is a legitimate production state; runtime null-guards (Objects.requireNonNull) mitigate NPE risk
CollocateQueryHelper.HitRecord record: 'String collocateLemma' — the Javadoc inline comment says '/* nullable */' but no @Nullable annotation is applied; annotation is missing
Map<String, Object> response maps throughout — fastjson2 constraint, noted wontfix

Handler decomposition is consistent: SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers each own one HTTP concern; WordSketchApiServer is a thin route-registration layer
SemanticFieldExplorer is explicitly documented as a coordination facade — owns single-seed exploration, delegates multi-seed to MultiSeedExplorer and comparison to CollocateProfileComparator
Options-object pattern applied consistently: ExplorationOptions (shared base), SingleSeedExplorationOptions (extends with nounsPerCollocate), FetchExamplesOptions — each adds exactly what it needs

ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreRequest is called only from handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti (one of three exploration handlers) — the abstraction covers one handler; handleSemanticFieldComparison explicitly documents why it does not use it

RelationType enum holds both routing types (SURFACE, DEP) and semantic types (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) in a single enum — acknowledged T4 wontfix, but creates a conceptual mixing
GrammarConfigSerializer is in api/ (moved from config/ to keep config/ free of fastjson2) — the design decision is explicit and documented
Dependency direction is clean and layered: model/ ← config/ ← exploration/ ← api/; query/ ← api/; utils/ ← all; viz/ ← api/ indirectly
No production code cycles: model/ has no project imports, config/ only imports model/, query/ only imports model/ and utils/
api/GrammarConfigSerializer imports config/ (api/ → config/) — correct downstream→upstream direction

model/SharingCategory.java Javadoc references 'pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api.ExploreResponseAssembler' in its @link — documentation dependency from model/ to api/ (upstream→downstream) but not a code import
exploration/SemanticFieldExplorer class Javadoc references '{@link pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api.ExplorationHandlers}' — documentation coupling from exploration/ to api/; not a code import
ExploreResponseAssemblerTest in model/ test package imports api/ExploreResponseAssembler — test-layer coupling, not production code

HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader reads System.getProperty("cors.allow.origin", DEFAULT_CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN) at call time, not at class load — explicitly documented 'so per-test overrides take effect immediately'; no boot-order fragility
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES is a static final Map initialized with compile-time literals — no external dependencies, safe at any load order
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor performs I/O (opens BlackLab index) — documented as intentional with a class-level Javadoc note; declares throws IOException so callers receive a checked exception

Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand registers the shutdown hook BEFORE constructing the server, with an AtomicReference that is null-checked on use — boot sequence is explicitly ordered and safe if construction throws
SemanticFieldExplorer constructor calls deriveNounCqlConstraint(grammarConfig), builds CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer — all pure computation, no I/O or environment reads
GrammarConfigLoader is a stateless static-method-only class — no class-level state to initialise, no boot-order dependencies
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor performs I/O at construction time (intentional, documented) which means tests and code that want to construct one must have a live index. This is an acknowledged constraint of the BlackLab lifecycle model, not a bug, but it limits testability and creates a hard dependency at construction time rather than at first use.

evidence: MainTest covers 6 CLI dispatch scenarios: no-args, help, unknown command, missing args for each subcommand — CLI path is validated
CorpusQueryHandlersTest covers 5 validation paths: body too large, invalid JSON, missing query, blank query, bracket depth — all 400/413 error paths for /api/bcql
HandlersTest covers handler validation (400 errors) for sketch, concordance, explore, explore-multi — only error paths, no happy-path handler tests with stub executor returning data
WordSketchApiServerTest starts a real server on port 18080 with a stub executor — tests route registration and health endpoint, but exploration/sketch endpoint happy paths are not exercised
ExploreResponseAssemblerTest tests edge building and response population with known data — good coverage of the serialization layer
SemanticFieldExplorerTest, MultiSeedExplorerTest, CollocateProfileComparatorTest cover the exploration algorithms with mocked QueryExecutor — algorithm logic well tested
BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest exists but is @Disabled (requires live index) — acknowledged wontfix
No tests for VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial HTTP layer: mode validation, size limits, JSON parse errors — RadialPlotTest only covers SVG rendering
All JSON endpoints return 'status': 'ok' as the first field — consistent success envelope
Error responses always use {'error': '...'} — consistent error envelope distinct from success

/api/semantic-field/examples uses 'adjective' and 'noun' parameters; /api/concordance/examples uses 'seed' and 'collocate' — different vocabulary for analogous parameters (both fetch examples for a word pair)

/api/semantic-field/examples returns 'adjective' and 'noun' in the response body; /api/concordance/examples returns 'seed' and 'collocate' in the response body — parameter names differ between sibling endpoints
/api/visual/radial returns image/svg+xml; all other endpoints return application/json — intentional but creates a distinct interaction model (no 'status' field in the response)
/api/bcql returns 'top' and 'total_results'; /api/concordance/examples returns 'top_requested' and 'total_results' — different field names for the same semantic concept (requested limit)

/api/semantic-field/explore-multi response includes 'seed_count'; /api/semantic-field/compare response includes 'seed_count' but also 'adjectives_count', 'edges_count' — fine-grained counts only on comparison endpoint
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc explicitly states the security model: 'All endpoints are unauthenticated — access control is provided by the host firewall and the loopback-only bind'
Server binds to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() in start() — all 12 endpoints are reachable only via 127.0.0.1, consistently
No endpoint has auth middleware; no endpoint is exempted from a non-existent auth layer — authorization is uniformly absent by design

The Javadoc security note warns: 'Any change to the bind address (e.g., to 0.0.0.0 for container deployment) must be accompanied by an authentication or network-level access-control layer' — the risk of future misconfiguration is explicitly documented
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The single-origin CORS policy (DEFAULT_CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN = 'http://localhost:3000') is hardcoded and overridable only via a JVM system property at startup; there is no runtime configuration or validation that the allowed origin is restricted. This is a minor gap: if the server is deployed with cors.allow.origin set to a permissive wildcard, the loopback security model's documentation warning applies but the code provides no enforcement.

strengths: Packages correspond exactly to domain capabilities with no catch-all or 'misc' directories — api, config, exploration, model, query, indexer, utils, viz each have a clear charter
Package-private visibility enforces subsystem boundaries: all HTTP handlers, MultiSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator are hidden behind their respective package surfaces
model/package-info.java documents the record-vs-class split and explains why ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult are classes, making structural intent transparent to newcomers
SemanticFieldExplorer Javadoc explicitly names its role as a coordination facade, preventing developers from misunderstanding its scope
ExploreResponseAssembler centralises all model-to-JSON translation, keeping HTTP response assembly away from both model and exploration packages
high_level_elegance.issue_character: A single split-placement issue: pure data option records live in exploration/ while result DTOs of the same conceptual tier live in model/, creating a small inconsistency that a developer following the model/ convention would not expect.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase clearly maps to 80+ (solid, developer can explain why each package exists) and approaches 90 (clear ownership with minor boundary blur). The packages are domain-aligned, access control is intentional, and design rationale is documented. The score stays at 86 rather than 90 because a developer learning the codebase would not confidently predict that ExplorationOptions belongs in exploration/ while ExplorationResult belongs in model/ — the split is consistent with exploration-algorithm specificity versus cross-cutting DTOs, but the line is subtle and undocumented compared to the model/ package-info.java.

strengths: All HTTP handler classes use the plural -Handlers suffix consistently — a developer seeing SketchHandlers can correctly predict the naming of ConcordanceHandlers and CorpusQueryHandlers
Enum label() + toString() pattern applied identically in RelationEdgeType, SharingCategory, and PosGroup — stable API serialization keys are consistent across all three enums
Package-private access modifier used consistently for implementation classes — api/, exploration/ both enforce public surface minimisation through the same mechanism
Record types used for pure data throughout model/ and exploration/; the two non-record classes in model/ are explicitly documented with rationale in package-info.java
camelCase/PascalCase Java naming conventions applied uniformly — no snake_case or Hungarian notation anywhere
convention_outlier.issue_character: A single isolated naming convention outlier in the utils package: LogDiceCalculator deviates from the -Utils suffix used by all other utility classes.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: Convention consistency is strong across this codebase — patterns for handler naming, enum serialization, visibility, and type representation are applied uniformly. The score reaches 90 (strong, mostly consistent with minor style islands that don't cause real confusion) because the only outlier is the -Calculator vs -Utils suffix for one utility class. This is a minor friction point that would not mislead a developer about behavior, but it does create a slight 'why is this one different?' moment in the utils package.

strengths: HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single consistent exception-to-HTTP-status mapping applied identically across all 12 API endpoints
GrammarConfigLoader explicitly documents its two-tier exception contract in createDefaultEnglish() Javadoc — a developer consuming this method knows exactly what to catch
FetchExamplesOptions validates constructor arguments eagerly and throws IAE immediately, preventing invalid option state from propagating
@Nullable annotation on RelationConfig.computeDeprel() and CqlUtils.extractConstraintAttribute communicates the null contract at the type level without forcing Optional overhead
error_consistency.issue_character: Mixed 'not found' signaling in the config layer — GrammarConfig.getRelation() returns Optional while RelationConfig.computeDeprel() returns @Nullable, creating a minor inconsistency in how 'absent value' is expressed within the same subsystem.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The error strategy is well-structured at the api/ boundary (consistent IAE→400 mapping) and the config/ loading path (documented exception tiers). The score lands at 78 (mixed, developer encounters inconsistencies that can't fully be trusted) because the config layer uses two distinct 'not found' signals (Optional vs @Nullable) without a documented rationale for the difference, and because ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples degrades silently to a fallback that produces different results without exposing the fallback path through an error — even though 'fallback: true' is set in the response, a client parsing only the results would not detect the quality difference.

strengths: QueryExecutor method names disambiguate four distinct query modes with precise vocabulary: executeSurfacePattern vs executeCollocations vs executeBcqlQuery vs executeDependencyPattern
SemanticFieldExplorer API method names (exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed, compareCollocateProfiles) communicate the operation AND the mode in a single readable token
ConcordanceHandlers internal record ConcordanceExamplesRequest uses domain-layer field names (noun, adjective) with explicit Javadoc explaining the seed→noun / collocate→adjective rename
parseSeedSet, parseRelationConfig, parseExplorationParams in ExplorationHandlers all follow a consistent parse-verb-noun pattern and match their return types
naming_quality.issue_character: Small cluster of method names that are either vague ('base'), compound ('AndFormat'), or that understate output scope (computeCollocateIntersection returns a full ExplorationResult, not just an intersection), concentrated in the handler and exploration-algorithm layers.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Naming quality is generally strong — domain vocabulary is used precisely and method names distinguish subtle behavioural differences. The score lands at 82 (solid but with recurring moments of imprecision) rather than 88+ because three non-trivial method names misrepresent their scope or behaviour: buildBaseExploreResponse overpromises narrowness, executeAndFormatCollocations signals two responsibilities, and computeCollocateIntersection understates its output. These are not trivial helpers; they are core dispatch paths where name clarity matters.

strengths: QueryExecutor single-impl interface explicitly acknowledged in Javadoc as a testability seam — honest about the abstraction's purpose rather than over-claiming
SemanticFieldExplorer documented as coordination facade in its Javadoc — its role as delegator rather than algorithm owner is transparent
ExploreResponseAssembler centralises all exploration-to-JSON translation in one class with clear static methods — the abstraction reduces duplication across three exploration handler methods
RelationPatternBuilder as a pure static utility class adds real leverage by centralising all BCQL pattern-substitution logic used by at least four callers
Option record hierarchy (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions composing base) avoids wide parameter lists in callers without creating indirection overhead
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Two isolated cases where the abstraction bends to caller convenience: a Map<String,Object> catch-all parameter in buildBaseExploreResponse and a 5-parameter testing seam that exposes internal pattern strings.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The abstractions in this codebase are generally well-calibrated — each layer adds genuine value (QueryExecutor for testability, SemanticFieldExplorer as stable facade, ExploreResponseAssembler for assembly isolation). The score is 83 (solid but with a few layers that create minor friction) rather than 90 because the Map<String,Object> extraParams in buildBaseExploreResponse bypasses the type discipline maintained elsewhere, and the 5-parameter testing overload in SemanticFieldExplorer exposes internal implementation strings that a well-designed seam would keep encapsulated. Neither issue is severe, but both represent places where abstraction fitness slipped to serve short-term convenience.

strengths: Single JSON library (fastjson2) used throughout the entire codebase — no competing JSON parsers
Version properties (${blacklab.version}, ${fastjson.version}, ${junit.version}) ensure both BlackLab artifacts stay in sync
logback-classic correctly scoped as runtime — does not pollute compile-time classpath
jspecify chosen for @NonNull/@Nullable annotations rather than heavier alternatives (Guava, Lombok) — minimal-footprint annotation library
No transitive utility dependencies (no Apache Commons, no Guava) — functionality implemented in-house where simple enough (MathUtils, CqlUtils)
dependency_health.issue_character: No structural dependency issues. The sole concern is the BlackLab resolution from a raw GitHub URL, which is documented and wontfixed.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is minimal, purposeful, and internally consistent — no duplicate libraries, no heavy dependencies for light use, and no version drift. The score reaches 91 (strong, mostly healthy) rather than higher only because of the BlackLab raw GitHub URL, which is a known fragility that the team has documented and deferred. A developer working on this project can trust the dependency set to serve their needs without conflicts or bloat.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer algorithm phases extracted into well-named private helpers (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) — a developer can read the main method as a pipeline
CollocateProfileComparator single-pass min/max/sum accumulation with documented rationale for the second variance pass — proportionate solution for the problem
CqlUtils.injectLemmaConstraint and substituteAtPosition are tightly scoped, do one thing, and have clear parameter semantics
VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial uses a flat sequential parse — no nested conditionals for JSON field extraction
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Two isolated implementation quality issues: a Pattern recompiled on every RelationConfig.computeDeprel() call, and CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens with nested while loops and multiple i-increment paths that require careful tracing to verify correctness.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Local implementation quality is generally clean — the extraction level is well-calibrated, helpers are focused, and algorithm phases are clearly separated. The score settles at 80 (solid but with recurring friction) because CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens requires non-trivial mental effort to verify index management correctness across its nested loops, and RelationConfig.computeDeprel() recompiles a Pattern on every call — both are specific but real cases where a developer must work harder than they should. The BiFunction orchestration in SketchHandlers is unusual for Java but readable in context.

strengths: ExplorationHandlers → SemanticFieldExplorer boundary is explicit: typed option records cross the seam, raw Maps stay in the handler layer
SemanticFieldExplorer documented delegation pattern (facade to MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator) makes the seam roles unambiguous
WordSketchApiServer constructor injection resolves all handler seams at construction time — no dynamic dispatch or post-construction wiring
FetchExamplesOptions, ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions provide typed seam objects that communicate exactly what crosses boundaries between handler and exploration layers
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Two seam design choices that create implicit contracts: ExploreResponseAssembler mutates a pre-built map rather than returning one (implicit side effect), and populateComparisonResponse accepts HTTP-layer parameters that do not belong in a model-to-presentation translator.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The seams between major layers (handler → explorer → executor) are clean and explicit. The score lands at 83 (mostly good seams with minor friction at a few boundaries) because the ExploreResponseAssembler API requires callers to follow an implicit 'build partial map, then pass for mutation' protocol, and the populateComparisonResponse signature leaks HTTP parameter values into what should be a pure model-to-JSON translation. Neither issue causes incorrect behaviour, but both create handoff contracts that a developer must infer rather than read.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer class Javadoc provides a genuine algorithm trace with examples, h2 sections, and design rationale — developer-informing rather than code-restating
HttpApiUtils class Javadoc explicitly justifies the co-location of two concerns with a structural argument — design explanation, not boilerplate
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() Javadoc explicitly documents the two-tier exception contract and explains when to use it vs load() — this is actionable developer guidance
QueryExecutor interface Javadoc precisely disambiguates four similarly-named execute methods — adds real value for a 6-method interface where name confusion is likely
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: Minor and isolated: one performance regression (Pattern recompile) and a small set of private helper Javadocs that restate method names without adding insight.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The code is predominantly purposeful — class-level and public-API Javadocs consistently add real developer value, and there are no generic domain names, no identical error-handling boilerplate, and no pass-through wrappers. The score lands at 85 (strong, mostly clean) rather than 90 because GrammarConfigLoader's private helper comments add ceremony without insight (a developer reading those Javadocs learns nothing beyond the method name), and the Pattern.compile recompilation in computeDeprel() is a characteristic AI-generation error of omission. Both issues are isolated to non-critical paths.

strengths: All recent structural migrations are complete: QueryResults in model/, GrammarConfigSerializer in api/, MathUtils.round2dp() replacing roundTo2DecimalPlaces, FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT deleted — no old stub classes or redirecting shims remain
GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys actively rejects old-format configs with a descriptive IAE — migration guard is enforced rather than passive
No stale migration TODO/FIXME comments found in the production source tree
The legacy /api/semantic-field route is documented in the server class Javadoc endpoint table and the handler Javadoc — its legacy status is textually communicated
incomplete_migration.issue_character: A single, isolated deprecation signal gap: the legacy API alias has comment-based deprecation but not annotation-based deprecation, meaning static analysis and IDE tooling cannot surface it.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase is in a clean post-migration state — no old shims, no dual-class patterns, no stale migration comments. The score lands at 89 (strong, mostly complete with minor legacy residue that doesn't cause confusion) rather than 95 because the legacy /api/semantic-field route is marked with a // comment rather than a @Deprecated annotation on handleSemanticFieldComparison. This is the only remaining migration signal gap, and it is isolated to a single handler method. A developer working with this code today will not be confused, but a developer extending or testing the handler method will not see the deprecation warning that annotation-based tooling would provide.

strengths: model/ is a clean, zero-dependency package holding all domain DTOs with a well-documented package-info.java explaining the two type categories
config/ is split into pure data carriers (GrammarConfig, RelationConfig) and loading/building machinery, preventing the data types from carrying IO deps
exploration/ owns the semantic-field algorithms (SemanticFieldExplorer facade, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) with no overlap into query/ or api/
utils/ contains only three stateless helpers (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, MathUtils) — no scope creep
GrammarConfigSerializer was moved from config/ to api/ to keep config/ free of fastjson2 dependencies — a clean separation of data vs presentation
package_organization.issue_character: Issues are isolated and localized: the most notable concern is that ExploreResponseAssembler and GrammarConfigSerializer live in api/ but are functionally presentation-layer serializers rather than HTTP handlers — acceptable given the package is cohesive but not entirely clean.
package_organization.score_rationale: The package structure is intentional and well-reasoned, with clear ownership boundaries and a dependency direction that flows model → config → exploration → api. A developer can navigate predictably and find things where expected. The one tension is api/ containing 11 files including two serializer utilities that don't touch HttpExchange — these could live in a model/serialization/ or api/serialization/ sub-package. This is a recurring friction point rather than a deep structural problem, placing the score in the 80s rather than 90s.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface documents all methods with precise contracts including null/empty behaviour, return semantics, and exception conditions
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() is annotated @Nullable and all callers correctly guard against null
ConcordanceHandlers documents the seed→noun / collocate→adjective rename in the record-level Javadoc, keeping the internal domain vocabulary explicit
ExplorationHandlers.parseRelationConfig clearly documents what causes IllegalArgumentException and how it is handled by wrapWithErrorHandling
GrammarConfig accessors use @NonNull and @Nullable annotations consistently, making the null contract machine-checkable
contract_coherence.issue_character: Issues are isolated: one stale @throws clause (GrammarConfigLoader) and one misleading error message (SketchHandlers) rather than a systemic pattern of contract divergence.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has strong contract habits — method signatures, annotations, and Javadoc align in the majority of cases. Two concrete deviations exist: GrammarConfigLoader.load's @throws Javadoc is stale after IOException→IAE refactoring, and SketchHandlers emits 'Unknown relation' for the wrong-type case where the relation is actually known. These are local, fixable issues rather than structural contract failures. The score of 82 reflects a solid but imperfect contract layer — developers are well-served most of the time but will encounter at least one documented contract that no longer matches the code.

strengths: Control flow in all five handler methods follows a clear guard-clause pattern: validate params early, then execute, then assemble response
MultiSeedExplorer separates the algorithm into named phases (fetchCollocatesPerSeed, identifyCommonCollocates, buildDiscoveredNouns, buildCoreCollocates) — intent is readable
CqlUtils.substituteAtPosition has clear pre-condition guards (null/blank checks) followed by a single transformation — no hidden state
ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison explicitly documents why it does NOT call buildExploreRequest — asymmetry is explained, not silent
logic_clarity.issue_character: Issues are local and isolated: one dead code branch (unreachable guard after loop) and one redundant isEmpty check; neither is systematically repeated.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: Most control flow in the codebase is direct and intention-revealing. The two logic issues found (dead guard, redundant check) are minor — they don't mislead and are immediately obvious on inspection. They are the result of defensive programming instincts rather than a structural logic problem. The score of 81 reflects code that serves developers well with rare moments of unnecessary complexity — on the 'solid but uneven' part of the scale.

strengths: Optional<RelationType> on RelationConfig.relationType() prevents accidental unguarded access to a potentially-absent value
sealed interface ConcordanceResult with two concrete permits enables exhaustive pattern-match coverage without runtime instanceof chains
@NonNull/@Nullable annotations on all public API surface methods (QueryExecutor, HttpApiUtils, ExplorationHandlers) provide machine-checkable contracts
Records used pervasively for DTOs eliminate mutable state and null-field risks in data carriers
GrammarConfig uses @NonNull on getVersion() and @Nullable on getConfigPath() — annotations match the semantic invariants (version is required, path is optional)
type_safety.issue_character: Issues are isolated: one missing @Nullable annotation on a record field that is documented nullable in a comment, and one @Nullable on a constructor parameter that conflates test-only-null with production-nullable.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase uses Java's type system well: Optional for optionality, sealed types for exhaustiveness, and annotation-based null contracts on public surfaces. The two gaps (missing @Nullable on HitRecord.collocateLemma, and the ambiguous @Nullable BlackLabIndex constructor parameter) are local and don't propagate risk broadly. The score of 81 reflects strong type discipline with a few soft spots that a developer would notice under static analysis.

strengths: Handler split into five focused classes from a single god-class is executed cleanly — each handler owns one domain with no overlap
SemanticFieldExplorer facade pattern is explicitly documented with the exact reason for the boundary (insulates HTTP layer from algorithm changes)
Config layer is cleanly separated into data carriers (GrammarConfig, RelationConfig) vs loading+building machinery (GrammarConfigLoader, RelationPatternBuilder, RelationUtils)
Options records compose cleanly: SingleSeedExplorationOptions wraps ExplorationOptions rather than duplicating its fields
design_coherence.issue_character: The main design tension is in ExplorationHandlers: buildExploreRequest is a shared-preamble helper called from only one of three handlers, creating an abstraction that doesn't carry its weight. This is a localized smell rather than a systemic pattern.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The overall design is coherent and intentional — decomposition is clean, ownership is clear, and the few asymmetries (buildExploreRequest, RelationType mixing) are either documented or flagged as known debt. The score of 80 reflects a developer would mostly be well-served but would encounter moments of 'why is this different here?' specifically around buildExploreRequest being used in only one of three handlers and the explicit asymmetry documented in handleSemanticFieldComparison.

strengths: No cycles in production code — model/, config/, exploration/, query/ are all dependency sinks relative to api/
QueryExecutor interface in query/ acts as a clean seam: api/ and exploration/ depend on the interface, not the concrete BlackLabQueryExecutor
utils/ has zero dependencies on any project module — it is a true utility layer
indexer/ is isolated from the runtime server path — only Main.java touches it for the indexing CLI command
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader separation prevents config/ from having upward dependencies
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Issues are mild documentation-layer concerns (Javadoc @link from upstream packages to downstream api/) rather than production code boundary violations.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The dependency architecture is cleanly layered with no production code cycles. A developer making changes in exploration/ or query/ can be confident they won't introduce unexpected ripples into api/. The two noted documentation @link references (model/ → api/, exploration/ → api/) are mild but do create an impression of reverse coupling when reading the Javadoc. These don't affect runtime behaviour but can mislead a developer exploring the architecture. Score of 84 reflects a well-structured codebase with only documentation-level rough edges.

strengths: System.getProperty for CORS origin is read at call time — overridable per-test without any boot-order constraint
Main's server startup explicitly sequences: shutdown hook registered first, then server constructed, then started — safe even if any step throws
All utility classes (CqlUtils, MathUtils, LogDiceCalculator, RelationPatternBuilder) are stateless — zero initialization concerns
GrammarConfigLoader is fully stateless — loading is always a local, explicit operation with no global side effects
SemanticFieldExplorer constructor is pure computation — no environment reads, no I/O, construction order is irrelevant
initialization_coupling.issue_character: No fragile initialization patterns are present; the one noted concern (I/O in BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor) is intentional and documented.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Initialization behaviour across the codebase is stable and well-reasoned. The only notable constraint is BlackLabQueryExecutor's constructor opening the index, which is intentional (documented) and doesn't create boot-order fragility — it simply requires a live index to construct. This is a known trade-off of BlackLab's lifecycle model and is handled explicitly by callers with proper exception handling. The score of 88 reflects a developer can import and construct any module without worrying about hidden dependencies or sequencing constraints.

strengths: All utility classes (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, MathUtils via MathUtils.round2dp) have dedicated unit tests
Grammar config loading is tested for valid configs, validation failures, duplicate IDs, missing version — full error matrix
Exploration algorithms (SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) are unit-tested with mock executors returning controlled data
CorpusQueryHandlersTest and HandlersTest use a lightweight PostMockExchange/MockExchange pattern without starting a real server — fast, isolated
MainTest provides the safety net for CLI argument parsing after recent additions
test_strategy.issue_character: The gap is a consistent pattern: handler classes are tested only for their validation (400-error) paths; happy-path handler tests with mock executors returning actual data are missing across ExplorationHandlers, VisualizationHandlers, and SketchHandlers.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The test portfolio is strong at the utility, algorithm, and validation layers — a developer can refactor CqlUtils, exploration algorithms, or grammar config loading with confidence. The blind spot is HTTP handler happy paths: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, and handleSemanticFieldComparison have no tests that verify they return correctly-shaped responses when given a working executor. VisualizationHandlers has no handler-level test at all. A developer making changes to response assembly or parameter handling in these handlers cannot verify correctness without running the server. Score of 72 reflects meaningful risk in the handler layer.

strengths: Consistent 'status': 'ok' envelope across all 10 JSON endpoints — a developer can always check this field
Consistent error shape {'error': '...'} with appropriate HTTP status codes (400, 413, 405, 500)
All exploration endpoints echo back the resolved parameters in a 'parameters' sub-object — a developer can verify what the server used
GET vs POST distinction is intentional and documented: GET for reads, POST for body-heavy operations (/api/bcql, /api/visual/radial)
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The inconsistency is systematic across two dimensions: (1) parameter naming for word-pair example endpoints diverges between semantic-field and concordance, and (2) the 'top' field naming is inconsistent between /api/bcql ('top') and /api/concordance/examples ('top_requested').
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The API has good macro-level consistency (status envelope, error format, GET/POST discipline) but a developer working across the example-fetching endpoints would encounter vocabulary drift: 'adjective'/'noun' in one endpoint and 'seed'/'collocate' in a sibling. The count-field naming ('top' vs 'top_requested') adds further inconsistency. These aren't severe enough to prevent use but would require a developer to check each endpoint's parameter names rather than predicting them from a seen pattern. Score of 77 reflects recurring but non-blocking inconsistency.

strengths: Security model is explicitly documented in the WordSketchApiServer class Javadoc — a developer knows the rationale, not just the mechanism
Loopback bind is enforced in code (InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress()) — the security property is not dependent on external configuration
All 12 endpoints are registered through the same registerGetHandler/registerPostHandler/registerHandler path — no special-case routing that could accidentally expose an unauthenticated endpoint
CORS origin is configurable via system property for test overrides but defaults to localhost:3000 — restricted by default
authorization_consistency.issue_character: No authorization inconsistency exists because the model is uniformly 'no auth'; the only concern is the absence of enforcement for the CORS origin constraint under misconfiguration.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: Authorization consistency is vacuously satisfied — all endpoints are uniformly unauthenticated, the security model is documented and enforced at the network-bind level. The score of 91 reflects a developer can trust the pattern is uniform with one minor friction: the CORS origin override path lacks validation that the configured value is not a wildcard, and a container deployment note suggests this could be misconfigured. This is a preventive concern rather than an existing inconsistency.

issues.summary: ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions are pure data records in exploration/ while peer result DTOs live in model/
issues.suggestion: Move ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, and FetchExamplesOptions to model/ and update imports in ExplorationHandlers, SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator. Update model/package-info.java to list them in the 'pure DTOs' section.
evidence: ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions are plain records with no algorithm logic, identical in character to DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile in model/
model/package-info.java lists 'pure DTOs' as records belonging in model/ but these option records are absent from that list despite fitting the description
api/ExplorationHandlers.java must import from both exploration/ (for option records) and model/ (for result records) to assemble a single request-response cycle

issues.summary: LogDiceCalculator uses -Calculator suffix while all peer utility classes use -Utils
issues.suggestion: Rename LogDiceCalculator to LogDiceUtils to match the -Utils convention used by all other static utility classes in the codebase. Update the single reference in CollocateQueryHelper.java.
evidence: Five utility classes end in -Utils: CqlUtils, MathUtils, RelationUtils, HttpApiUtils, RelationPatternBuilder (static-only final class)
LogDiceCalculator is a static-only final class with no state, identical in structure to MathUtils — only the suffix differs
A developer searching for 'math utilities' would look for MathUtils-style naming and might not find LogDiceCalculator

issues.summary: config/ uses Optional for GrammarConfig.getRelation() but @Nullable for RelationConfig.computeDeprel() with no documented rationale
issues.suggestion: Add a brief comment to computeDeprel() explaining why @Nullable is used rather than Optional (e.g. 'returns null rather than Optional to avoid boxing cost in per-relation hot path, or for JSpecify annotation compatibility'). If no strong reason exists, align to Optional<String> for consistency with getRelation().
evidence: GrammarConfig.getRelation(String id) returns Optional<RelationConfig> — callers must handle Optional
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() returns @Nullable String — callers receive a nullable primitive-like value
Both methods are in the same config layer and both signal 'absent value', but through different mechanisms — a developer must learn two patterns

issues.summary: ConcordanceHandlers falls back to proximity pattern when relation is not found, producing structurally different results without surfacing an error
issues.suggestion: Throw IllegalArgumentException when the relation ID is unknown ('Unknown relation: ' + req.relation()), consistent with ExplorationHandlers.parseRelationConfig() which throws IAE for unknown relations. Remove the fallback path and the associated 'fallback' response field. If fallback is intentionally required, document the contract in the handler Javadoc and consider returning 207 or adding a warnings array to the response.
evidence: When grammarConfig.getRelation(req.relation()) returns empty, resolvedQuery is null, fallback=true, and a proximity pattern '[]{0,5}' is used instead of the grammar-defined BCQL pattern
The response includes 'fallback: true' but returns HTTP 200 — an API client that checks only status codes or result counts would not detect the quality degradation
logger.warn logs the fallback, but nothing in the error-handling contract (wrapWithErrorHandling) would surface this to the caller as a recoverable error

issues.summary: buildBaseExploreResponse: 'base' is vague — the method builds a fully-structurally-complete response map that callers only augment with seed-specific keys
issues.suggestion: Rename to initExploreResponse or buildExploreResponseTemplate to communicate that the method creates a well-formed structural template (not a partial/incomplete object) that callers extend with seed-specific payload.
evidence: buildBaseExploreResponse(String relationType, int topCollocates, int minShared, double minLogDice, Map<String,Object> extraParams) returns a HashMap with 'status', 'relation_type', and 'parameters' — a complete structural template, not just a partial base
Callers augment the returned map with seed-specific fields (seed, seeds, seed_count) — these are semantic additions, not structural completion

issues.summary: executeAndFormatCollocations in SketchHandlers names two distinct responsibilities and returns Optional<ExecutedSketch> not suggested by 'execute'
issues.suggestion: Rename to fetchAndFormatRelationSketch or simply fetchRelationSketch (the Optional<ExecutedSketch> return already implies 'may be absent'). Alternatively split into two methods: one that executes and returns Optional<List<WordSketchResult>>, and one that formats.
evidence: Method both executes executeSurfacePattern(...) AND formats results into List<Map<String,Object>> collocations
Returns Optional<ExecutedSketch> — the Optional wrapper (for empty-result skipping) is not hinted by the execute-verb prefix
The private record ExecutedSketch wraps both the raw results and formatted collocations, suggesting the method boundary could be named after that concept

issues.summary: computeCollocateIntersection in MultiSeedExplorer understates scope: method builds a full ExplorationResult, not just an intersection set
issues.suggestion: Rename to exploreMultiSeedCollocates or buildMultiSeedExplorationResult to communicate that the method's output is a complete exploration result, not an intersection of sets.
evidence: Method returns ExplorationResult containing seeds, seedCollocScores, seedCollocFreqs, discoveredNounsList, and coreCollocatesList — a complete exploration result, not just an intersection
The intersection of common collocates is just one intermediate step in the method; the name implies the method's purpose is that step alone
Callers (SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed) receive the full result but have to know that 'intersection' actually means 'full multi-seed exploration'

issues.summary: buildBaseExploreResponse takes Map<String,Object> extraParams, bypassing type safety for caller-specific response fields
issues.suggestion: Remove the extraParams parameter. Let handleSemanticFieldExplore add 'nouns_per' directly to the returned map after calling buildBaseExploreResponse (the method already returns a mutable HashMap). Or extract a single-seed-specific response builder that includes nouns_per in its typed signature.
evidence: buildBaseExploreResponse(String relationType, int topCollocates, int minShared, double minLogDice, Map<String,Object> extraParams) — extraParams is a type-erased bag for arbitrary response additions
handleSemanticFieldExplore passes Map.of('nouns_per', exploreParams.nounsPerCollocate()) as extraParams — a typed int field wrapped in an untyped map
handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti passes Map.of() (empty) — the parameter exists for a single caller's use, invisible to the method signature

issues.summary: RelationConfig.computeDeprel() compiles Pattern.compile() on every call — should be a static final field
issues.suggestion: Extract the pattern as a private static final field: 'private static final Pattern DEPREL_PATTERN = Pattern.compile("deprel=[\"']([^\"']+)[\"']");' and reference it in computeDeprel().
evidence: computeDeprel() body: 'Pattern p = Pattern.compile("deprel=[\"']([^\"']+)[\"']");' — local variable created on every invocation
computeDeprel() is called from SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType() inside a loop over all relations, and from buildRelationResponse() — repeated recompilation per request
Pattern.compile is documented as expensive; the pattern string is constant

issues.summary: CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens has nested while loops with multiple i-increment paths that make correctness hard to verify
issues.suggestion: Extract a private helper method skipQuotedString(String pattern, int i) that advances past a quoted string and returns the new index. This isolates the quoting logic and makes the outer and inner loop bodies read as single-purpose sequences without interleaved increment management.
evidence: The outer while loop has three branches: '[' (enters inner while), '"' (enters inner while), else (explicit i++). Only the else branch has an explicit i++ — the '[' and '"' branches rely on inner loops to advance i

Inside the '[' inner while: the '"' sub-branch increments i explicitly (skip opening quote, loop over content with i++, then falls through to inner i++ skipping the closing quote), while the '[' and ']' branches do not increment — all rely on the inner loop's trailing i++

When ']' closes at depth==0, code does 'i++; break' — then the outer while starts the next iteration from the advanced i without any additional increment. This is correct but requires tracing three levels of increment to verify

issues.summary: parseBcqlRequest uses a double-null-check chain to parse the optional 'top' integer field
issues.suggestion: Simplify to: 'int resolvedTop = obj.containsKey("top") && obj.getIntValue("top") > 0 ? obj.getIntValue("top") : 10;' or extract a utility method parseOptionalInt(JSONObject, String, int) analogous to HttpApiUtils.parseIntParam.
evidence: 'Integer top = obj.get("top") != null ? obj.getIntValue("top") : null; int resolvedTop = (top != null && top > 0) ? top : 10;' — two ternary chains to handle absent vs invalid vs valid
fastjson2 JSONObject has containsKey() that can replace the first null check: 'int resolvedTop = obj.containsKey("top") ? Math.max(1, obj.getIntValue("top")) : 10;'

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse and populateComparisonResponse mutate a caller-supplied map rather than returning a new one
issues.suggestion: Change populateExploreResponse to return Map<String,Object> rather than mutating a parameter: 'public static Map<String,Object> buildExploreResponse(ExplorationResult result)'. Callers then merge the returned map into their response: 'response.putAll(ExploreResponseAssembler.buildExploreResponse(result))'. For populateComparisonResponse, move the seeds/topCollocates/minLogDice parameters to the caller — the assembler should only translate ComparisonResult to Map.
evidence: populateExploreResponse(Map<String,Object> response, ExplorationResult result) — method signature takes the map to populate, implies the caller must have already built a base map

populateComparisonResponse(Map<String,Object> response, ComparisonResult result, Set<String> seeds, int topCollocates, double minLogDice) — same mutation pattern, plus HTTP-layer parameters (seeds, topCollocates, minLogDice) that should be derived from ComparisonResult or handled by the caller

In ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison: caller creates new HashMap(), calls populateComparisonResponse with it — the implicit protocol 'create empty map, pass to populate' is not captured by the return type

issues.summary: populateComparisonResponse takes HTTP-layer parameters (seeds, topCollocates, minLogDice) that should stay in the handler layer, not cross into the assembler
issues.suggestion: Move the seeds/topCollocates/minLogDice response fields to ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison after calling the assembler: 'response.put("seeds", new ArrayList<>(seeds)); response.put("top_requested", topCollocates); etc.' Reduce populateComparisonResponse signature to (Map<String,Object>, ComparisonResult).
evidence: populateComparisonResponse(Map<String,Object> response, ComparisonResult result, Set<String> seeds, int topCollocates, double minLogDice) — seeds, topCollocates, minLogDice are request parameters, not exploration results
The assembler adds 'seeds', 'top_requested', 'min_logdice' to the response map from these parameters — this is handler-layer knowledge (what parameters were requested) leaking into the assembler
populateExploreResponse by contrast only takes ExplorationResult — consistent with a pure model-to-JSON translator

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader private helper Javadocs restate method names without adding insight
issues.suggestion: Remove or collapse the Javadocs on parseAndValidateVersion and parseRelations to a single-line comment if any clarification is needed. Reserve full Javadoc for methods where the name alone does not communicate non-obvious constraints or policies.
evidence: '/** Extracts and validates the 'version' field from the root JSON object. */' on parseAndValidateVersion — the method name parseAndValidateVersion and the parameter JSONObject root already communicate this
'/** Parses all relation entries from the JSON root into the provided mutable lists/maps. */' on parseRelations — restatement of the method signature

By contrast, validateNoLegacyKeys Javadoc ('Rejects deprecated top-level keys that must not appear in the config.') adds value by naming the policy ('must not') and the class of keys ('deprecated top-level')

issues.summary: RelationConfig.computeDeprel() compiles Pattern.compile on every call instead of using a static final field
issues.suggestion: Add 'private static final Pattern DEPREL_ATTR_PATTERN = Pattern.compile("deprel=[\"']([^\"']+)[\"']");' as a static field on RelationConfig (records can have static fields) and replace the local Pattern.compile call with DEPREL_ATTR_PATTERN.
evidence: Body of computeDeprel(): 'Pattern p = Pattern.compile("deprel=[\"']([^\"']+)[\"']");' — new Pattern object created on every call
computeDeprel() is called inside a loop in SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType() (once per relation per request) and in buildRelationResponse() — repeated recompilation per request lifecycle
The pattern string is constant — it never varies based on the RelationConfig state

issues.summary: Legacy /api/semantic-field route marked with // comment, not @Deprecated annotation on the handler method
issues.suggestion: Add a /** @deprecated Use /api/semantic-field/compare instead. */ Javadoc on the inline route registration block, or extract the legacy route registration into a private method registerLegacySemanticFieldRoute() annotated with @Deprecated. This makes the deprecation visible to IDE inspections and static analysis without changing behaviour.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer line 95: '// @deprecated - legacy alias for /api/semantic-field/compare; kept for backward compatibility.' — using a line comment rather than the Java @Deprecated annotation
ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison method has no @Deprecated annotation — IDEs and static analysis will not flag callers or test references as using a deprecated path

The canonical handler handleSemanticFieldComparison is not itself deprecated (correct — it's used by both paths), but the legacy route registration at /api/semantic-field lacks machine-readable deprecation signaling

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler and GrammarConfigSerializer are serialization utilities in api/ but have no HttpExchange dependency
issues.suggestion: Create src/main/java/pl/marcinmilkowski/word_sketch/api/serialization/ and move both files there. Update 19 callers (handler classes and tests) with: (1) mv ExploreResponseAssembler.java → api/serialization/, (2) mv GrammarConfigSerializer.java → api/serialization/, (3) update package declarations, (4) rerun `mvn compile` to validate imports. Alternatively, keep them in api/ and accept the minor impurity — the package is otherwise coherent.
evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler.java has no com.sun.net.httpserver import — it converts ExplorationResult/ComparisonResult to Map<String,Object>
GrammarConfigSerializer.java has no com.sun.net.httpserver import — it converts GrammarConfig/RelationConfig to JSONObject
Both are public final utility classes whose sole callers are handler classes in api/

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.load @throws clause says IOException for invalid config but validation now throws IllegalArgumentException
issues.suggestion: Update the load() Javadoc: '@throws IOException if the file cannot be read' and add '@throws IllegalArgumentException if the config JSON is structurally invalid (missing version, duplicate relation IDs, unknown relation_type)'. Also update fromReader() similarly.
evidence: load() Javadoc: '@throws IOException if the file cannot be read or the config is invalid'
parseAndValidateVersion(), validateNoLegacyKeys(), parseRelations() all throw IllegalArgumentException for validation failures
Main.java catches IAE and IOException separately with different user messages, confirming the distinction matters to callers

issues.summary: SketchHandlers throws 'Unknown relation: X' when the relation exists but is the wrong type (SURFACE vs DEP)
issues.suggestion: Split the condition: if (rel == null) throw new IAE("Unknown relation: " + relationId); if (rel.relationType().orElse(null) != relationType) throw new IAE("Relation '" + relationId + "' is not a " + relationType + " relation");
evidence: handleRelationQueryForPattern: 'if (rel == null || rel.relationType().orElse(null) != relationType) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown relation: " + relationId)'
When rel is non-null but the wrong type, the error message 'Unknown relation' is factually incorrect — the relation is known, just inapplicable for the requested type

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler.serializeEdge rounds inline with Math.round instead of MathUtils.round2dp used by all sibling methods
issues.suggestion: Replace with MathUtils.round2dp(edge.weight()): m.put("log_dice", MathUtils.round2dp(edge.weight()));
evidence: serializeEdge: 'm.put("log_dice", Math.round(edge.weight() * 100.0) / 100.0)'
formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates, formatAdjectiveProfile all call MathUtils.round2dp()
Numerically equivalent but creates visual inconsistency that makes a reviewer wonder if the difference is intentional

issues.summary: Dead code: 'if (scores.length == 0)' guard is unreachable because scores is only populated from existing map entries
issues.suggestion: Remove the dead guard. If defensive coverage is desired, initialise with: 'double sum = 0.0, maxScore = 0.0, minScore = 0.0;' and let the loop overwrite. This makes the zero-scores case explicit rather than handled by a dead branch.
evidence: scores is built as: 'double[] scores = nounScores.values().stream().mapToDouble(Double::doubleValue).toArray()'
nounScores is the value from adjectiveProfiles.entrySet() — any entry in adjectiveProfiles was put there by at least one '.put(noun, r.logDice())' call, guaranteeing at least one score

The subsequent 'if (scores.length == 0) { maxScore = 0.0; minScore = 0.0; }' can never execute; maxScore and minScore are left as NEGATIVE_INFINITY/POSITIVE_INFINITY in a reachable-zero scenario that does not exist

issues.summary: SketchHandlers: redundant !specificDeprel.isEmpty() check on a string that is always non-empty when non-null
issues.suggestion: Simplify to: 'if (specificDeprel != null)' — the isEmpty() check adds visual noise without protecting against any reachable condition.
evidence: 'String specificDeprel = parts.length > 2 ? parts[2] : null'
'if (specificDeprel != null && !specificDeprel.isEmpty())'
parts[2] is a segment from path.split("/") — URL path segments are never empty strings after splitting on "/" for a well-formed path; the isEmpty() check is never true when specificDeprel is non-null

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.HitRecord: collocateLemma field is documented nullable in a comment but lacks @Nullable annotation
issues.suggestion: Add 'import org.jspecify.annotations.Nullable;' and annotate: 'record HitRecord(..., @Nullable String collocateLemma, ...)'. Remove the inline comment since the annotation is self-documenting.
evidence: record HitRecord(..., /* nullable */ String collocateLemma, ...) — the comment acknowledges nullability
No @Nullable annotation is present on the field, leaving nullability invisible to static analysers and callers using the record
In scoreHits, collocateLemma is accessed with a null check, confirming it is indeed nullable at runtime

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper constructor annotates BlackLabIndex as @Nullable, conflating test-only-null with production-nullable
issues.suggestion: Remove @Nullable from the constructor parameter and instead add a package-private test-only constructor overload: 'CollocateQueryHelper() { this.index = null; }' with a comment explaining its test-only purpose. This keeps the production constructor's @NonNull contract honest while still enabling test subclasses.
evidence: CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex index) Javadoc: 'Null is accepted to support test subclasses that override getTotalFrequency and executeCollocateSearch. Production callers must always supply a non-null index.'
The @Nullable annotation at the constructor level signals to static analysers that null is a legitimate value, but the Javadoc then says production must never pass null
Runtime guards (Objects.requireNonNull) in individual methods protect against NPE, but the constructor signature misrepresents the production contract

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreRequest is a shared-preamble helper with only one caller (handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti)
issues.suggestion: Inline buildExploreRequest into handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti and delete the ValidatedExploreRequest record. The method currently parses seeds, relation, and exploration params — these three calls inline clearly: 'String seedsRaw = requireParam(params, "seeds"); RelationConfig config = parseRelationConfig(params); ExplorationParams ep = parseExplorationParams(params);'. This makes the handler self-contained and removes an abstraction that creates confusion (why doesn't explore use this?).
evidence: buildExploreRequest() is called exclusively from handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti
handleSemanticFieldExplore calls parseRelationConfig and parseExplorationParams separately (not via buildExploreRequest)
handleSemanticFieldComparison explicitly documents why it does NOT call buildExploreRequest
The ValidatedExploreRequest record is a single-use type whose only purpose is packaging the return value of a single-caller method

issues.summary: model/ and exploration/ Javadoc use @link references to api/ classes, creating apparent upstream→downstream documentation coupling
issues.suggestion: In SharingCategory.java, replace the api/ @link with a plain prose description: 'Used for type-safe serialization of the category field in API responses.' In SemanticFieldExplorer.java, change 'serves as the stable API surface for {@link ...ExplorationHandlers}' to 'serves as the stable API surface for HTTP handler classes in the api layer.' This removes the apparent reverse coupling without losing the intent.
evidence: SharingCategory.java Javadoc: 'Used by {@link pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api.ExploreResponseAssembler#formatAdjectiveProfile}'
SemanticFieldExplorer.java class Javadoc: 'serves as the stable API surface for {@link pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api.ExplorationHandlers}'
These are documentation-only references (not import statements) but mislead architectural readers into thinking model/ and exploration/ know about api/

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers has no happy-path tests: handleSemanticFieldExplore/ExploreMulti/Comparison are tested only for 400 errors
issues.suggestion: Add happy-path tests to HandlersTest using a stub SemanticFieldExplorer (can be a lambda or anonymous class): (1) test handleSemanticFieldExplore with ?seed=house&relation=adj_predicate and verify response has 'status':'ok', 'seed':'house', 'relation_type', 'parameters' fields; (2) test handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti with ?seeds=theory,model; (3) test handleSemanticFieldComparison with ?seeds=a,b. Use MockExchange from the existing test infrastructure.
evidence: HandlersTest covers handleSemanticFieldExplore_missingSeed_returns400 and similar validation cases only
No test exercises handleSemanticFieldExplore with a stub SemanticFieldExplorer that returns an ExplorationResult — response shape, field names, and parameter echoing are untested
WordSketchApiServerTest starts the server but does not issue GET /api/semantic-field/explore with valid params
Recent changes (buildSingleSeedRequest eliminated, logic inlined) have no regression test coverage for correct response assembly

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers has no handler-level tests: mode validation, size limit, and JSON error paths are untested
issues.suggestion: Add VisualizationHandlersTest using PostMockExchange (same pattern as CorpusQueryHandlersTest): (1) invalid JSON body → 400; (2) unknown mode value → 400 with 'Unknown mode' error; (3) body over 65536 bytes → 413; (4) valid request → 200 with SVG content type; (5) MAX_RADIAL_ITEMS truncation (send 50 items, verify 40 are rendered).
evidence: RadialPlotTest exercises RadialPlot.renderFromItems directly — the SVG generation is tested
handleVisualRadial has additional logic: JSON parse error handling, 'Unknown mode: X' IAE for invalid mode, MAX_REQUEST_BODY_BYTES limit, MAX_RADIAL_ITEMS truncation — none of these are tested
The mode validation added in the recent changes (throws IAE for unknown modes) has no regression test

issues.summary: Parameter names diverge between /api/semantic-field/examples (adjective/noun) and /api/concordance/examples (seed/collocate) for analogous word-pair lookups
issues.suggestion: Standardise on 'seed' and 'collocate' across both endpoints (these are already used by the concordance endpoint and by the explore endpoints). In ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExamples, change the param names from 'adjective'/'noun' to 'collocate'/'seed'. Update the response body to match ('collocate', 'seed'). Update the Javadoc. This is a breaking API change — add a compatibility note and schedule for next minor version.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExamples: 'String adjective = HttpApiUtils.requireParam(params, "adjective"); String noun = HttpApiUtils.requireParam(params, "noun")'
ConcordanceHandlers.parseConcordanceExamplesRequest: 'String noun = HttpApiUtils.requireParam(params, "seed"); String adjective = HttpApiUtils.requireParam(params, "collocate")'
Both endpoints return examples for a word pair in context — a developer discovering /api/semantic-field/examples first would use 'seed'/'collocate' by analogy with explore endpoints and get a 400

issues.summary: /api/bcql response uses 'top' for the applied limit; /api/concordance/examples uses 'top_requested' — same concept, different names
issues.suggestion: Standardise to 'top' across both endpoints: in ConcordanceHandlers, change response.put("top_requested", req.top()) to response.put("top", req.top()). Note this is a minor breaking change for clients reading that field.
evidence: CorpusQueryHandlers.buildBcqlResponse: 'response.put("top", req.top())'
ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples: 'response.put("top_requested", req.top())'
Both represent the same semantic: the maximum result count requested by the caller
